content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus 1

**Chapter:** Leviticus 1 (17 verses)
**Arbitrator:** SOPHIA
**Total disagreements:** 17 across 14 verses
**Verses with full agreement:** vv. 7, 10, 14 (with minor notation exceptions)

---

## v. 1 — Subject and structure of the opening

- **EMET:** "And he called to Moshe, and the LORD spoke to him from the tent of meeting, saying:"
- **LOGOS:** "And he called to Moses, and the LORD spoke to him from the tent of meeting, saying,"
- **PNEUMA:** "The LORD called to Moses and spoke to him from the tent of meeting, saying:"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** EMET and LOGOS agree on two distinct verbal clauses: (1) "And he called to Moses" — subject unstated, (2) "and the LORD spoke to him." PNEUMA collapsed the two verbs into a single subject-explicit sentence: "The LORD called to Moses and spoke." This is a substantive divergence from the MT structure. The Hebrew *va-yiqra el-Mosheh va-yedabber YHWH* presents two separate verbal actions — the calling and the speaking — with the divine name appearing only in the second clause. This two-clause structure has theological weight: the unstated subject of the first verb (*va-yiqra*) reflects the scribal tradition of the small *aleph*, signaling a distinctive mode of divine speech. SOPHIA rules with EMET and LOGOS, preserving the two-clause MT structure. Name form: "Moses" (English convention, as in LOGOS and PNEUMA) over EMET's "Moshe" — the project uses English name forms throughout.

**SOPHIA's text:** "And he called to Moses, and the LORD spoke to him from the tent of meeting, saying:"

---

## v. 2 — Addressee formula: "sons of Israel" vs. "people of Israel"

- **EMET:** "Speak to the sons of Yisra'el and say to them"
- **LOGOS:** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them"
- **PNEUMA:** "Speak to the people of Israel and say to them"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew is *bnei Yisra'el* — literally "sons of Israel" — the standard covenantal address formula throughout the Pentateuch. EMET and LOGOS are in agreement on the literal rendering. PNEUMA's "people of Israel" is an interpretive paraphrase that replaces the covenantal kinship term with a sociological descriptor. The project convention is to render established covenant formulas literally. SOPHIA rules with EMET and LOGOS: "sons of Israel." Name form follows English convention ("Israel" not "Yisra'el").

**SOPHIA's text:** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them"

---

## v. 2 — Offering clause: presence or omission of "from the livestock"

- **EMET:** "When a man of you brings near an offering [*qorban*] to the LORD, from the livestock, from the herd or from the flock, you shall bring near your offering."
- **LOGOS:** "When any man among you brings an offering to the LORD, from the livestock — from the herd or from the flock — you shall bring your offering."
- **PNEUMA:** "When any of you brings an offering to the LORD, bring your offering from the herd or from the flock."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Three distinct issues: (a) PNEUMA omitted *min ha-behemah* ("from the livestock"), which is present in the MT and functions as the generic category before the specific subdivisions; (b) PNEUMA shifted the second "bring" to an imperative ("bring your offering") rather than a declarative, obscuring the MT's legal formulation; (c) EMET used "brings near" (from *qarav*) twice, which is etymologically precise but stylistically heavy. LOGOS's balance — preserving the livestock clause with natural English flow — is preferred. SOPHIA also retains the *qorban* transliteration at first occurrence per EMET's note. "Any man among you" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is preferred over EMET's more literal "a man of you" — the indefinite sense is clearer in English.

**SOPHIA's text:** "When any man among you brings an offering [*qorban*] to the LORD, from the livestock — from the herd or from the flock — you shall bring your offering."

---

## v. 3 — Acceptance clause rendering (*li-rtsono*)

- **EMET:** "for his acceptance [*ratson*] before the LORD"
- **LOGOS:** "to find acceptance before the LORD"
- **PNEUMA:** "that your approach may be accepted before the LORD"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew *li-rtsono* is literally "for his acceptance/favor" — the suffix *-o* refers to the offerer, not to God. All three translators recognize this but handle it differently. EMET is most literal, retaining the third-person suffix and adding a transliteration gloss. LOGOS adds an infinitive clause ("to find") not present in the MT. PNEUMA shifts to second person ("your approach") and introduces the concept of "approach" (*qarav*) from the *qorban* root — theologically rich, but interpretive. SOPHIA rules for a close reading of the MT: "for his acceptance before the LORD." This preserves the third person, the *ratson* sense of favor, and avoids adding words not in the Hebrew. The *ratson* transliteration gloss is noted here but not retained in the running text, as *qorban* was already glossed in v. 2.

**SOPHIA's text:** "for his acceptance before the LORD"

---

## v. 3 — "offer" vs. "bring" for the approach to the tent

- **EMET:** "a male without blemish he shall bring it near — to the entrance of the tent of meeting he shall bring it near"
- **LOGOS:** "he shall offer a male without blemish. He shall bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting"
- **PNEUMA:** "bring a male without blemish. Bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** EMET's double "bring near" is etymologically precise (both verbs are *hiqriv*, from *qarav*) but syntactically awkward in English. LOGOS uses "offer" for the first instance and "bring" for the second, introducing a differentiation the Hebrew does not make. PNEUMA shifts to imperative mood throughout, which flattens the legal-declarative register of the MT. SOPHIA renders the verse with natural flow, using "bring" consistently, maintaining third-person declarative register.

**SOPHIA's text:** "he shall bring a male without blemish. He shall bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting"

---

## v. 4 — Atonement preposition: "on his behalf" vs. "for him"

- **EMET:** "to make atonement [*le-khapper*] on his behalf"
- **LOGOS:** "to make atonement on his behalf"
- **PNEUMA:** "to make atonement for him"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew is *le-khapper alav* — literally "to make atonement upon him / over him / for him." EMET and LOGOS agree on "on his behalf." PNEUMA uses "for him," which is slightly more personal in English. PNEUMA's note argues that "for him" captures atonement as personally effective rather than merely transactional. Both are defensible. SOPHIA rules with EMET and LOGOS ("on his behalf") for consistency with the rendering of *alav* established in Genesis and Exodus legal texts, where "on his behalf" is the project convention.

**SOPHIA's text:** "to make atonement on his behalf"

---

## v. 5 — Animal term: "the bull" vs. "the son of the herd"

- **EMET:** "he shall slaughter the son of the herd before the LORD"
- **LOGOS:** "he shall slaughter the bull before the LORD"
- **PNEUMA:** "he shall slaughter the bull before the LORD"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew is *ben ha-baqar* — literally "son of the herd," meaning a young male bovine (bull/steer). EMET preserves the literal phrase. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "the bull" as the natural English equivalent. SOPHIA rules with LOGOS and PNEUMA: "the bull" is the accurate, idiomatic rendering; "son of the herd" is technically precise but would require a note every time it appears, and the consensus two-to-one preference for "the bull" reflects sound translation judgment.

**SOPHIA's text:** "he shall slaughter the bull before the LORD"

---

## v. 5 — Blood-application: "all around" vs. "sides of the altar" vs. "all sides"

- **EMET:** "dash the blood against the altar all around"
- **LOGOS:** "dash the blood against the sides of the altar"
- **PNEUMA:** "throw it against all sides of the altar"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew is *ve-zarqu et-ha-dam al ha-mizbeach saviv* — literally "and they shall dash the blood upon the altar, all around." Three issues: (a) verb: EMET and LOGOS use "dash" (*zaraq*); PNEUMA uses "throw." SOPHIA rules for "dash" — it captures the forceful, directed application of blood that *zaraq* denotes, and distinguishes it from the lighter *nazah* (sprinkle). (b) spatial phrase: EMET's "all around" is a literal rendering of *saviv*; LOGOS "sides of the altar" is slightly interpretive but natural; PNEUMA "all sides" combines both. SOPHIA rules with LOGOS: "the sides of the altar" is the idiomatic English equivalent of *saviv* in this context and is less redundant than "all sides." (c) PNEUMA also drops "bring the blood" (*ve-hiqrivu et-ha-dam*), omitting a priestly action. SOPHIA restores it.

**SOPHIA's text:** "the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall bring the blood and dash the blood against the sides of the altar"

---

## v. 6 — "flay" vs. "skin" vs. "skin"

- **EMET:** "And he shall flay the burnt offering and cut it into its pieces."
- **LOGOS:** "And he shall skin the burnt offering and cut it into its pieces."
- **PNEUMA:** "He shall skin the burnt offering and cut it into pieces."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew is *ve-hiphshit et ha-olah ve-nitach otah li-netacheha* — "and he shall flay the burnt offering and cut it into its pieces." EMET uses "flay" (a more technical term for removing skin); LOGOS and PNEUMA use "skin." Both are correct — *hiphshit* means to remove the skin. "Skin" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the more current English term; "flay" (EMET) is traditional and vivid. SOPHIA rules for "skin" as the more widely understood English verb in this context. Additionally, PNEUMA omitted "its" before "pieces" (*li-netacheha* — "into its [the animal's] pieces"); SOPHIA restores the possessive with EMET and LOGOS.

**SOPHIA's text:** "And he shall skin the burnt offering and cut it into its pieces."

---

## v. 8 — Arrangement phrase: "upon the wood" vs. "on the wood" / connector "and its fat" vs. "the fat"

- **EMET:** "the pieces, the head and the fat, upon the wood which [is] upon the fire upon the altar"
- **LOGOS:** "the pieces, the head and the fat, on the wood that is on the fire that is on the altar"
- **PNEUMA:** "the pieces — the head and the fat — on top of the wood that is on the fire on the altar"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Minor structural and word-choice differences. (a) PNEUMA adds "on top of" — *al* in the MT can mean "upon/on top of," but the addition of "on top of" is an interpretive clarification not strictly necessary. SOPHIA uses "on" (LOGOS) for naturalness. (b) PNEUMA's em-dash punctuation is a stylistic choice; SOPHIA follows LOGOS's comma structure. (c) "which is" (EMET) vs. "that is" (LOGOS/PNEUMA): SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "that is" is the modern English relative clause convention in formal prose.

**SOPHIA's text:** "the pieces — the head and the fat — on the wood that is on the fire on the altar"
(Adopting PNEUMA's em-dash for clarity of the parenthetical list, but "on the wood" not "on top of the wood.")

---

## v. 9 — "entrails" vs. "inner organs"

- **EMET:** "its entrails and its legs he shall wash with water"
- **LOGOS:** "its entrails and its legs he shall wash with water"
- **PNEUMA:** "the inner organs and the legs he shall wash with water"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew is *et qirbo ve-et-kra'av* — "its entrails/inward parts and its legs." EMET and LOGOS agree on "entrails." PNEUMA uses "inner organs," which is more clinical but not wrong. SOPHIA rules with EMET and LOGOS: "entrails" is the established liturgical/traditional rendering of *qerev* in the sacrificial context and is consistent with the parallel section (v. 13). Consistency across the three parallel sections is a priority.

**SOPHIA's text:** "its entrails and its legs he shall wash with water"

---

## v. 11 — "dash its blood" vs. "throw its blood"

- **EMET:** "the sons of Aharon, the priests, shall dash its blood against the altar all around"
- **LOGOS:** "the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall dash its blood against the sides of the altar"
- **PNEUMA:** "Aaron's sons the priests shall throw its blood against all sides of the altar"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Same set of issues as v. 5. (a) "dash" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "throw" (PNEUMA) — SOPHIA rules "dash" for *zaraq* consistently. (b) "all around" vs. "sides of the altar" vs. "all sides" — SOPHIA rules "sides of the altar" (LOGOS) as in v. 5. (c) Name form: EMET uses "Aharon" (transliteration); LOGOS uses "Aaron" (English form); PNEUMA uses "Aaron's sons" (possessive inversion). SOPHIA uses the English form "Aaron" and the full phrasing "sons of Aaron, the priests" to match v. 5.

**SOPHIA's text:** "and the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall dash its blood against the sides of the altar"

---

## v. 13 — "bring all of it and burn" vs. "offer all of it and burn" vs. "bring all of it and burn"

- **EMET:** "the priest shall bring near the whole and shall burn [it] upon the altar"
- **LOGOS:** "the priest shall offer all of it and burn it on the altar"
- **PNEUMA:** "the priest shall bring all of it and burn it on the altar"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew is *ve-hiqriv ha-kohen et ha-kol ve-hiqtir ha-mizbechah* — "and the priest shall bring near the whole and shall burn [it] on the altar." Three renderings: EMET is most literal ("bring near the whole"); LOGOS substitutes "offer" for the bringing action; PNEUMA uses "bring all of it." SOPHIA rules for PNEUMA's "bring all of it" — it captures the *hiqriv* (bring near) action while being more natural than EMET's "bring near the whole." LOGOS's "offer" conflates the two priestly actions (*hiqriv* + *hiqtir*) and is slightly imprecise.

**SOPHIA's text:** "the priest shall bring all of it and burn it on the altar"

---

## v. 15 — Technique for bird head: "pinch off" vs. "wring off"

- **EMET:** "shall pinch off [*ve-malaq*] its head and shall burn [it] upon the altar"
- **LOGOS:** "shall wring off its head, and the priest shall burn it on the altar"
- **PNEUMA:** "shall wring off its head and burn it on the altar, draining its blood out against the side of the altar"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew *ve-malaq* describes a specific technique — removing the bird's head at the back of the neck by the priest's thumbnail without a blade. This is one of Leviticus's most specialized technical terms. EMET's "pinch off" is supported by the LXX *apoknisei* ("to nip/pinch"). LOGOS and PNEUMA use "wring off," which suggests a twisting motion. The rabbinic tradition (Mishnah Zevachim 6:5) describes *melikah* as a motion of the thumbnail from back to front — which is closer to a wringing/twisting action than a simple pinch. SOPHIA rules with LOGOS and PNEUMA: "wring off" better captures the wringing, twisting motion described in the rabbinic sources, even though the LXX supports EMET. The physical reality of the act governs over lexical cognates.

Additionally: PNEUMA's rendering of the blood-drainage is notable — "draining its blood out against the side of the altar" — and is more natural than EMET's "its blood shall be drained out against the side of the altar." SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's more formal structure: "its blood shall be drained out against the side of the altar."

**SOPHIA's text:** "the priest shall bring it to the altar and wring off its head and burn it on the altar, and its blood shall be drained out against the side of the altar."

---

## v. 16 — Crop contents: "feathers" vs. "contents"

- **EMET:** "he shall remove its crop [*mur'ato*] with its feathers"
- **LOGOS:** "he shall remove its crop with its feathers"
- **PNEUMA:** "he shall remove its crop with its contents"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew *be-notsatah* — "with its feathers/plumage" — is a hapax. The root *natsah* relates to feathers, plumage, or (in some readings) filth/impurity. EMET and LOGOS agree on "with its feathers," supported by the LXX Vaticanus (σὺν τοῖς πτεροῖς, "with the feathers/wings"). Targum Onkelos reads the term as "filth/excrement," and PNEUMA's "contents" reflects a mediating paraphrase. SOPHIA rules with EMET and LOGOS and the LXX: "with its feathers" — the plumage reading is supported by the strongest external witnesses and the plain sense of the root. EMET's *mur'ato* transliteration is retained for the hapax.

**SOPHIA's text:** "he shall remove its crop [*mur'ato*] with its feathers"

---

## v. 17 — "shall not divide it" vs. "shall not divide it completely" vs. "shall not sever it completely"

- **EMET:** "he shall not divide it"
- **LOGOS:** "shall not divide it completely"
- **PNEUMA:** "shall not sever it completely"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew is *lo yavdil* — literally "he shall not divide/separate it." No qualifier (like "completely") appears in the MT. EMET preserves the simple negation exactly. LOGOS and PNEUMA both added "completely," interpreting the action as a partial split rather than a total separation — which is accurate as a description of what happens (the bird is splayed but not halved), but "completely" is not in the text. Additionally, PNEUMA uses "sever" where the Hebrew *yavdil* (from *badal*, "to divide/separate") is better rendered "divide" (EMET/LOGOS). SOPHIA rules with EMET: *lo yavdil* is rendered "shall not divide it" — the simple negation without added qualifiers. The procedure itself (tearing open by the wings while the body remains connected) makes the meaning clear without the word "completely."

**SOPHIA's text:** "he shall tear it open by its wings but shall not divide it"

---

## Summary Table

| Verse | Key Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA's Ruling |
|-------|-----------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | Subject of *va-yiqra* | Two clauses, unstated subject | Two clauses, unstated subject | One clause, LORD explicit | Two clauses (EMET/LOGOS) |
| 2 | "sons of" vs. "people of" Israel | sons of Yisra'el | sons of Israel | people of Israel | sons of Israel (EMET/LOGOS) |
| 2 | Livestock clause omitted | present | present | absent | present (EMET/LOGOS) |
| 3 | *li-rtsono* acceptance clause | for his acceptance | to find acceptance | that your approach may be accepted | for his acceptance (EMET) |
| 3 | Verb for approach to tent | "bring near" (×2) | "offer" + "bring" | imperative "bring" | "bring" declarative (modified LOGOS) |
| 4 | Atonement preposition | on his behalf | on his behalf | for him | on his behalf (EMET/LOGOS) |
| 5 | "son of the herd" vs. "bull" | son of the herd | the bull | the bull | the bull (LOGOS/PNEUMA) |
| 5 | Blood: "all around" vs. "sides" | all around | sides of the altar | all sides | sides of the altar (LOGOS) |
| 6 | "flay" vs. "skin" | flay | skin | skin | skin (LOGOS/PNEUMA) |
| 8 | "on top of" vs. "on" | upon the wood | on the wood | on top of the wood | on the wood (LOGOS) |
| 9/13 | "entrails" vs. "inner organs" | entrails | entrails | inner organs | entrails (EMET/LOGOS) |
| 11 | Blood: "dash" vs. "throw" | dash; all around | dash; sides | throw; all sides | dash; sides (LOGOS) |
| 13 | "bring near" vs. "offer" vs. "bring" | bring near the whole | offer all of it | bring all of it | bring all of it (PNEUMA) |
| 15 | *ve-malaq*: "pinch off" vs. "wring off" | pinch off | wring off | wring off | wring off (LOGOS/PNEUMA) |
| 16 | Crop contents: "feathers" vs. "contents" | feathers | feathers | contents | feathers (EMET/LOGOS + LXX) |
| 17 | "divide" + "completely" qualifier | shall not divide it | shall not divide it completely | shall not sever it completely | shall not divide it (EMET) |

---

*Logged by SOPHIA — Leviticus 1, 17 verses, 17 disagreements across 14 verses.*
